answer:
**The Bṛhaspati Smṛti (Chapter 1)** anatomizes the court of justice (*dharmādhikaraṇa*) as a "ten-limbed instrument" (*daśāṅgopakaraṇa*) presided over by the king.[^1]

This instrument is first declared at verse 1.60cd ("this is the ten-limbed instrument over which the king presides"), referenced again at 1.83ab ("presides over this ten-limbed instrument"), and its limbs are listed across verses 1.84–85ab:

> Of these limbs, the king is the head (*śiras*), and the appointed officer (*prāḍvivāka*) is called the mouth (*mukham*);  
> the assessors (*sabhāsthānaka*) are the arms (*bāhu*), the Smṛti the hands (*pāṇi*), and the accountant (*mudrā*) and scribe (*kāraṇa*) the shanks (*jāṅghe*).[^2]  
> Gold, fire, and water are the eyes (*netre*), the heart (*hṛdayam*) is the king's conscience (*buddhi*), and his own officer (*svādhikṛta*) is the feet (*pāda*).[^3]

The remaining limbs (e.g., function of each) are elaborated in subsequent verses (1.86–90).[^4]

[^1]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-a30c28).
[^2]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-db3b97).
[^3]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-c074ad).
[^4]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, Chapter 1, [paragraph](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-508073).
